_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Functions
summary: Define typed inputs and a returned result.
refresher: A function declaration states its return type, name, and parameter types. `return` supplies a compatible result. Passing by const reference avoids copying a large input that should not change.

blocks.body: A function declaration states its return type, name, and parameter types. `return` supplies a compatible result. Passing by const reference avoids copying a large input that should not change. Define typed inputs and a returned result.

C++ normally passes an argument by value, which creates a separate parameter value. An ampersand in a parameter type makes it a reference to the caller?s object. Add `const` when the function should read that object without copying or changing it.

blocks.caption: The two arguments initialize typed parameters, and the selected integer returns to main.
Use a const reference for a large read-only input.
blocks.right: int total(const std::vector<int>& values) { /* reads original */ }

blocks.body: The wrong signature makes a new vector for the call. The corrected parameter refers to the caller?s vector, while const prevents modification through that reference. Small types such as int are usually clearer and cheap to pass by value.

blocks.title: Follow the data
blocks.body: A non-void function that reaches its end without a return has undefined behavior when the caller expects a result.

blocks.prompt: What is the return type here?
blocks.why: The type written before the function name declares the returned value type.
The argument initializes n, and the returned multiplication result is six.

blocks.title: Functions checklist
items: Use const references for read-only large values.
